title: Rabbit housing and run design
subtitle: Minimum space, predator-proofing, and run enrichment for kept rabbits.
excerpt: Rabbits need far more space than most small hutches provide. Getting the housing right from the start prevents boredom, injury, and predator losses.
sourceNotes: RWAF (Rabbit Welfare Association and Fund) housing guidelines 2023. RSPCA rabbit care advice. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Rabbits (2019).

glossaryTerms.definition: A network of tunnels and chambers that wild rabbits dig and live in. Understanding warren behaviour helps explain why kept rabbits need space to run, hide, and explore.
Rigid welded wire mesh with small square gaps, typically 6 mm or 13 mm. Far stronger than chicken wire and used on rabbit runs to resist foxes, badgers, and rats.
A horizontal apron of mesh pegged flat on the ground around the outside edge of a run. It stops predators digging under the perimeter without requiring a buried barrier.

content.text: Wild rabbits roam large territories and live in a
warren
with many exits. Kept rabbits have the same physical and social needs. Housing that is too small causes stereotypic behaviour, obesity, and spinal problems.
Minimum space (UK guidance)

The RWAF recommends a minimum footprint of 3 m x 2 m per pair, with 1 m of height. This allows a full binky (a leaping twist that signals contentment). A traditional single hutch does not meet this. A hutch should be attached to a permanent run, not used alone.
Rabbits should have access to the run at all times, not just for an hour a day. Lock rabbits into the hutch sleeping area at night only if the run is not fully predator-proof.
Predator-proofing
Use
hardware cloth

rather than chicken wire. Chicken wire has large gaps and is easy to pull apart. Foxes can reach through gaps and kill rabbits without entering the run. Use 13 mm welded mesh on all sides, the roof, and any floor panels.
Fit a
skirt mesh

around the outside perimeter. Peg it flat on the ground and weigh it with stones or turf. This stops foxes and badgers from digging under the run edge. A buried L-shaped barrier (30 cm down, 30 cm outward) is the alternative if you prefer a permanent installation.
Check all locks daily. Foxes learn to work simple sliding bolts. Use two-point fastenings on every door and hatch. Padlocks are not needed, but twist-and-lift latches are more secure than clips.
Weather protection

Rabbits are vulnerable to heat above 25 C and to rain-driven damp. Provide a solid-sided shelter within the run that is large enough for all rabbits to enter at once. The hutch sleeping area must be fully draught-free but ventilated.
In summer, move the run to shade in the afternoon. Frozen water bottles placed in the run help rabbits cool down. Signs of heat stress include laboured breathing and a stretched-out flat posture.
In winter, pack the sleeping area with extra hay. Hay is the best insulator and is also the rabbit's main food source. Newspaper under the bedding layer adds a small amount of extra warmth.
Run enrichment
Rabbits need mental stimulation. A bare run causes frustration and repetitive behaviours. Add items that encourage digging, foraging, and hiding.
A large plant pot or trug filled with soil or sand for digging.
Cardboard tunnels and cardboard boxes. Replace when wet or soiled.
Willow or apple wood twigs for chewing. Avoid treated or painted wood.
A raised platform (raised at least 30 cm) so rabbits can sit above ground level and survey the area.
Hay scattered on the floor as well as in a rack. Foraging for hay mimics natural grazing.
Rotate enrichment items so the environment stays novel. Rabbits are curious and benefit from small changes to their space.
